step.title: Step 9
step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='Retract LEFT to pull Red block toward agent')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 28, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Path extended to reach Red block. The Red block is at the end of the path but hasn't been collected yet (still at cols 42-45). Need to extend further to collect it.
- Goal model: Navigate the agent to connect to colored blocks in the order shown in bottom panel: Red → Green → Blue.
- Action model: UP/DOWN moves agent vertically by 6 rows. RIGHT extends the path rightward. Need to test what happens when path reaches a colored block.
- Plan: Press RIGHT more to fully collect the Red block, then move to collect Blue.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 27 of about 61 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The Red block is still at cols 42-45, unchanged. The path extends to col 46, past the Red block. But the Red block hasn't been collected. This is strange - the Green block was collected earlier when the path reached it.
Wait, let me reconsider. Earlier, the Green block was at cols 42-45. When I extended the path to reach it, the Green block moved to cols 18-21 (next to the agent). But now the Red block is at cols 42-45 and the path extends past it, but Red hasn't moved.
Maybe the issue is the ORDER. The bottom panel shows Pink → Red → Green → Blue. Green was collected first (wrong order), and now Red can't be collected because it's not the right order. Or maybe the game requires a specific sequence.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the bottom panel. It shows: Pink, Red, Green, Blue. Maybe this is the order in which blocks should be collected. But I collected Green first (accidentally), which was wrong. Now the game might be stuck because I collected them in the wrong order.
Hmm, but the game didn't give any error or reset. Let me think about this differently.
Maybe the collection mechanic works differently. When the path first reached the Green block, it was collected. But now the Red block is NOT being collected even though the path reaches it. 
Wait - maybe the collection only happens when the path FIRST reaches a block from the left. The Green block was collected because the path extended through it for the first time. But the Red block... let me check if the path actually touches the Red block.
Looking at the path tiles: the path extends to col 46, and Red is at cols 42-45. So the path goes THROUGH the Red block. But Red didn't get collected.
Hmm, maybe the issue is that the Green block was collected because it was the FIRST block the path reached. But now, since Green was already collected, the game expects Red to be collected next. But Red isn't being collected because... maybe the path needs to reach it from a different angle?
Or maybe the collection only happens when the path END is at the block, not when it passes through. Let me check: the path extends to col 46, past the Red block. So the path END is past Red. Maybe I need to retract the path so the END is exactly at the Red block.
Let me try retracting the path with LEFT until the path end is at the Red block (cols 42-45).
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the whole mechanic. Looking at the earlier sequence:
1. Agent was at rows 30-35 (aligned with Green)
2. Extended RIGHT, path reached Green at cols 42-45
3. Green block moved to cols 18-21 (collected)
4. Later, agent moved UP to rows 18-23 (aligned with Red)
5. Extended RIGHT, path reached Red at cols 42-45
6. Red block did NOT move
The difference: in step 3, the Green block moved. In step 6, the Red block didn't move. What's different?
Oh wait, maybe the issue is that the Green block WASN'T collected by the path. Let me re-examine. When I first extended the path to reach Green, the path went to col 46. Then I pressed LEFT multiple times to retract the path. During those LEFT presses, the Green block moved from cols 42-45 to cols 36-39 (according to one segmentation read). Then later, the Green block is at cols 18-21.
So maybe the Green block moves LEFT when I press LEFT? That would be a different mechanic. Let me think...
Actually, I think I need to reconsider. The Green block at cols 18-21 is right next to the agent (cols 11-16). When I moved the agent UP, the Green block stayed at rows 31-34. So it doesn't move with the agent.
Let me check: when did the Green block move from cols 42-45 to cols 18-21? It was at cols 42-45 initially. Then I pressed LEFT 7 times, and the segmentation showed it at cols 36-39. Then I pressed UP twice, and it was at cols 18-21.
Wait, that doesn't make sense. The Green block shouldn't move when I press UP. Unless... the Green block moves when the path retracts?
Let me reconsider: maybe when the path retracts (LEFT), it "pulls" the collected block toward the agent. So:
1. Path reaches Green → Green is "grabbed"
2. Path retracts (LEFT) → Green moves left with the path
3. Eventually Green ends up next to the agent
This would explain why Green moved from cols 42-45 to cols 18-21 when I pressed LEFT multiple times.
If this is the mechanic, then to collect Red, I need to:
1. Extend RIGHT to reach Red (done)
2. Retract LEFT to pull Red toward the agent
Let me try pressing LEFT to see if Red moves!

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Path extends past Red block but Red hasn't been collected. KEY INSIGHT: The Green block was collected by first reaching it with the path, then retracting with LEFT which pulled it toward the agent. So the mechanic is: extend path to grab a block, then retract to pull it to the agent.
Plan: Retract with LEFT to pull the Red block toward the agent.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Retract LEFT to pull Red block
predict(expect_change=True, note='Retract LEFT to pull Red block toward agent')
action(['LEFT'] * 5)

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("=== After LEFT retraction ===")
print("Colored blocks:")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] in ['R', 'b', 'N'] and n['pixels'] == 16:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        r = min(rows); c = min(cols)
        if r < 50:
            print(f"  color={n['color']} rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
=== After LEFT retraction ===
Colored blocks:
  color=R rows 19-22, cols 18-21
  color=b rows 25-28, cols 42-45
  color=N rows 31-34, cols 18-21
